_ , VOL_ 11_ APRIL 1911 No. 9

THE PARLOUR LIBRARY.

* When the office of the * Northern Star ’ was raided and wrecked
. in 1797, two compositors named D. Simms and J. Doherty were thrown -

out of employment. They at once formed a partnership and started a
press of their own, and so continued up to 1803, when a separation took
place. Ifor the next two years, we find their individual imprints on
various hooks, and then the name of Doherty disappears for ever. In
1807, Simms entered into a fresh partnership with a McIntyre, and we
meet, for the first time their joint imprint. This firm grew and flourished
chiefly by the publication of educational works, amongst which, those
of Prof. Thomson, the father of Lord Kelvin, take a prominent place.
Nearly forty years afterwards, John Simms and James McIntyre, the
sons of the original partners, conceived the idea of printing a series of
standard copyright works of fiction at a cheap, uniform rate, under the
title of “ The Parlour Novelist?’ thus forestalling the smany similar
series we know to-day. It was a bold undertaking on the part of two
printers in a small provincial town as Belfast then was, but the dis-

notice that John Simms, the survivor, has just passed away (9{'11'

| 9093 years.

thing must be left to conjecture. No 119 is ‘* Maurice Tiernay,”

hich had run through the Dublin University Magazine as a serial 1n
1850. This, the first edition of the work was published without the
guthor’s name, for fear, IPitzpatrick suggests that the public would
chink he was writing himself out. So also did “.Sir Jasper Carew,’”
which came to an end it the same magazine in June, 1854. It was pub-
lished as No. 123 of the ‘‘ Parlour Library ’ with a preface dated Janu-
ary, 1855. It is in this form, that the original editions of these two novels
by Lever, are to he found. With the divorce of Simms and McIntyre
from the ¢ Parlour Library ’’ our interest ceases, but the series existed
down to 1862. There were 276 volumes published in all; a full list of
which will be fouund in the English Catalogue. The stereotype plates

were purchased by Geo. Routledge and Sons, who continued to issue the

better known and longer lived volumes until quite l'ecen.tly-, but without
the green backs of the once famous Parlour ILabrary, which was so-

popular with our fathers.and grandfathers. _

éi In N. and Q., second series, 1, 24, it was alleged that she was 4 of
EE-{ ‘“ Phillips,”” but no evidence was given of this, only hearsay. - th
: : In a printed foreword in G. Colman and B Thomas’s ‘‘ Poems by

Eminent Ladies of Great Britain and Ireland,’”’ London (1755 and 1773), { ur
containing some of Mrs. Grierson’s poems, she 1s descrlbed as ‘‘of
‘ Kilkenny.”” In contradiction to this again I have heard that she
came from the Co. Cavan, but that ‘¢ Phillips ’’ was certainly her
maiden name, and it is alleged there is written evidence to prove it. | i
She died in December, 1732, and was buried in St. John’s Churchyard, | 3
Dublin,. on the 4th of that month. Her death is briefly mentioned in § .
Faulkner’s Dublin Journal and in the Dublin Weekly Intelligence, bub #

' = in the Parish Register only her name is given under the date 4ih A
8 December. I have always understood that she was ‘the first wife of s
’ | George Grierson and that his second wife was a sister of James Blow, 4 ;-
: S e printer, of Belfast, from whose issue the present members of the Grer- “§ 7
. son family are descended, I believe. il 1 at

o ongly confirmed by a MS. note, on my copy opposite the verse above

quoted in contempm ary handwriting not unlike that of Swift, indeed.
This 18 it: ¢ Miss Crawley, afterwards wife to Grierson the Printer.’’ -
Where and when Geo. Grierson married ‘‘ Constantia >’ I do not know. -E';q,-.
The question then remains was ‘‘ Constantia,”’ Miss Crawley? It i
1ooks very like it, buv it conflicts with the ‘‘ Phillips ’ story. I would « _
bo glad if furcher light could be thrown on the point. There are several
Crawleys given in the index of marriage license bonds for the diocesses
of Ossory, Ferns, and Leighlin as well as Phillipses. It is also notable
that in 1797 a Jane Crawley married a. Robert Grierson.

There remains as a possibility that Geo. Grierson: was married three
| times. E. R. Mc. C. DIX.

THE ‘ YOUNG IRELAND ”’ PRESS IN 1848.

“Tis a great day and glorious, oh! public for you. This October
fifteenth, eighteen-forvy-two,”” sang poor Clarence Mangan, when the
* Nation’s first number ”> appeared. For five years it gave expression
t all that was brightest and best in the literature of Ireland. One very
hostile critic admitted that ‘‘ Some of the most beautiful songs that
ever breathed treason were published in the columns of this paper, by
clever impetuous young men, who sought with the light of genius to
kindle the flame of rebellion.”” When ‘‘the pale browed enthusiast,
_Dams the latest loved ”’ passed away all too soon, his place was filled

its last number appeared, the ‘‘leaders’ in which were writtey it '
Marvin and Reilly. On the 10th June, 1848, the ‘‘ Irish Tribune * Wag |
started by two young medical students, R. D. Williams and Keviy
O’Doberty, that is their names were entered at the Custom Hoyge ag
proprietors, but it was wellknown that the sinews of war came from ]
elsewhere. Amongst its contributors were Stephen Meany, afterwargs 1
a Fenian convict, Dr. Antisell, John Savage, Michael Doheny, De Courcy 1
Young, and W. T. Meyler, whilst Carleton’s ‘‘ Evil Eye ’’ commenneq 1
as a serial, but only three chapters appeared in its pages. On the 8 |
of July, befere_ the sixth weekly number had appeared, the proprietors i 1
- with Hoban, the printer, were arrested and the paper suppressed. ]
During their imprisonment Williams and O’Doherty issued the pros- ]
 pectus of a new weekly paper to be called the *f Newgate Calendar,” i
successor to the ‘‘ Tribune.”” This may have only been their fun as ]
nothing came of it. 4
_ On the 24th June there stvarted from the press whence issued the
“« United Irishman,” an- avowed continuation of it under the title of ]
tH ‘““ Insh. Felon,”’ of which the tone was even more warlike than its ]
predecessors. Tt was edited by ‘¢ Honest 7. John Martin, and am_ongat %)
the contributors were Fintan Lalor, Devin Reilly and ‘ Young !
Meagher of the Sword,” whilst Joseph Brennan acted as sub-editor. ]
It was no longer lived than the others, for it was seized by the police f |
; '-t_m 22nd July and came to an end. Martin, who, with Duffy was also ;
. arrested on 8th, as is well known, was sentenced to transportation for R
~ ten years, the others being more fortunate. The suspension of e :" (

13,699 1tems of which 1,429 relate to the Celtic languages. Many of
the books are of the greatest rarity and apart from its importance to the
gtudent of philology, the collection possesses considerable interest for
the bibliophile. For instance Prince Bonaparte had the only known
perfect copY of the first edition of ‘‘ Dowley’s Irish Cathecism ’ (Lou-
vain, 1663), with the title leaf, which is wanting in each of the other
chree copies, which have been traced of this very rare book.

TouaM PRINTING.—Mr. Dix’s excellent article in the February No.
reminds me that I have a little pamphlet envitled, ** Proceedings of the
Tuam Liverary Institute for the quarter ending 6th April, 1851, 12mo.,
pp. 44. It was published by Duffy, of Dublin, but as it bears no printer’s
name, I presume it was printed in Tuam. From it we learn that the
Society was established in December, 1850, and this 1s the record of
its first session, and a very creditable one it is. The inaugural address o
was delivered by Mr. J. O’Shaughnessy; Mr. T. M. Brennan followed gt
with a paper on the ‘ Steam Engine,” then Mr. J. Costello came with e S
one on ‘‘ The Naturai History of Man,” and finally Mr. Joseph Kjyle
discoursed on History.” | JOHN OF TUAM.

é %E E Alexander of Macedon was so familiar to the Irish that in one medieval,
